key_insight: The paper proposes OSET, in which plastic deformation in any solid starts from lattice-free oblate-spheroid transformation zones, and classical dislocations appear only as a collective limit of many such zones.
engineering_relevance: This reframes plasticity modeling for metals, ceramics, glasses, grain boundaries, and nanocrystals by providing a unified constitutive basis that does not require a periodic lattice.
method: Model each elementary plastic event as an Eshelby oblate-spheroidal inclusion with shear eigenstrain.
Show that one OSTZ gives a finite, non-singular elastic dipole, while a co-planar chain of OSTZs reproduces a Peierls-Nabarro dislocation.
Derive strength- and energy-related quantities, including Peierls stress, core energy, Frank-Read critical stress, and stacking-fault energy, from OSTZ geometry.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports validation against grain-boundary-sliding data, literature across metals, ceramics, and bulk metallic glasses, and a 41-system compilation, with dilatational eigenstrains matched within 2% and shear eigenstrains within 15%.
watch_item: The framework appears parameter-rich in geometric/eigenstrain assumptions, so its predictive reliability will depend on how well OSTZ parameters can be identified for a given material and loading state.
paper_title: Oblate Spheroid Excitation Theory: A Unified, Lattice-Free Foundation for Plastic Deformation from Which Dislocations Emerge as Collective Excitations
